result:
Somewhere around the fifteenth minute of staring at the ceiling, I began to suspect that the crack running above my bed was actually a river seen from space. The longer I looked, the more tributaries appeared: a delta of plaster feeding an ocean of off-white paint. I traced it with the same reverence my grandfather once traced the blue highways on his gas-station map of the United States, finger hovering, never quite touching the paper, as if the states might rearrange themselves out of shyness. That was the summer he taught me how to read topography by almost-touch: how a ridge feels like the back of a sleeping animal, how a valley exhales cool air even on a map. I never learned to fold the map back correctly; it remained a crumpled testament to everywhere we might have gone if the Buick hadn’t thrown a rod outside Tucumcari. We spent the night in the car anyway, windows cracked, radio whispering Patsy Cline through a veil of static. He said static was the sound of departed things still trying to speak. I was eight and thought he meant ghosts. Now I think he meant childhood, or maybe 1962.

The ceiling river doesn’t speak, but it moves if you watch long enough. Last week it delivered a flotilla of dust motes that drifted south-by-southwest, obeying a current I couldn’t feel. I half-expected to see tiny barges, maybe a crew of pocket-sized longshoremen waving infinitesimal flags. Instead I got the usual: the beige blur of insomnia, the soft click of the smoke detector blinking its microscopic eye. I once read that the human eye can detect a single photon, provided the retina is sufficiently dark-adapted. I’ve been testing this by refusing to turn on the lights when I get up to pee at 3:18 a.m. So far the darkness remains unbroken, but I swear the river glows, a faint bioluminescence like plankton in a midnight cove. I have never seen the ocean at night; I imagine it smells like refrigerator coils and vanished homework.

Insomnia is a kind of unpaid internship with the universe. You clock in, you sit there, you accomplish nothing tangible, yet you come away altered. My curriculum has included: the exact number of whistles the freight train makes at the Broad Street crossing (34, if you count the Doppler ghost); the way the neighbor’s porch light stutters like it’s relaying Morse code to my bedroom; the discovery that the word “insomniac” contains the word “minnow” if you squint and allow scrambled letters, which feels significant because minnows are river creatures and therefore under my jurisdiction now. I am the unofficial steward of this ceiling watershed. I keep expecting a delegation of spiders to arrive with tiny hardhats and a clipboard asking for environmental-impact statements.

There ought to be a bureau you can call for此类matters. A hotline where a calm voice answers, “Federal Bureau of Unclaimed Metaphors, how may I direct your call?” I would report: 1) A crack has become a river. 2) Time is pooling in the northwest corner of the bedroom—about three tablespoons’ worth, shimmering like mercury. 3) My childhood keeps washing up in the reeds: a plastic dinosaur, a ticket stub from a 1989 air-show, the smell of Coppertone and cut grass. The agent would thank me, assign a case number, and send a technician in a nondcript van. Two days later the river would be gone, filled with quick-drying spackle, sanded smooth, painted over in landlord white. I would sleep the sleep of the literal, the unhaunted, the firmly unmetaphorical. But nobody’s coming; the government is busy elsewhere, shoveling time into larger cracks.

So I stay enrolled. Last night the syllabus expanded: I learned that memory smells like pencil shavings mixed with the metallic breath of an old cassette deck. I remembered being twelve, recording songs off the radio, finger poised over the red REC button, praying the DJ wouldn’t talk over the intro. I remembered the first time I heard “River” by Joni Mitchell and how I misheard the line “I made my baby cry” as “I made my baby fire,” which seemed miraculously stupid and profound, as if I’d invented a new form of heartbreak. I remembered that rivers can be songs, and songs can be roads, and roads can be folded badly and stuffed into a glove compartment forever. I remembered that my grandfather died on a Tuesday, which is the most river-shaped day of the week, long and winding and mostly ignored.

The ceiling river knows all this. It absorbs every unvoiced remembrance, every half-dreamed dream. It is the catchment basin of whatever leaks out of me when the lights are off and the day’s performance has ended. I contribute: the anxiety about the dentist, the embarrassment from 1997 when I called the teacher “Mom,” the recurring fantasy in which I speak fluent French and rescue a small village from oblivion by translating their dying patois into bestselling children’s books. The river takes it, swirls it downstream, deposits it somewhere behind the light fixture. I picture an estuary of forgotten embarrassments, a sandbar of unwritten novels, a mangrove of lullabies I never sang to anyone.

Tonight I decide to contribute something intentional. I whisper into the dark: “Take this—my apology to every houseplant I’ve murdered, my gratitude to the unknown engineer who calibrated the refrigerator hum to the key of C, my promise that if I ever inherit a boat I will name her Tuesday and sail her up the plaster current until she reaches the stars.” The crack widens a nanometer, or maybe I blink. A singular photon, perhaps, escapes the detector’s blink. I feel it land on the surface of my pupil like a minnow nibbling at the meniscus of a real river, somewhere far from here, where the darkness is so complete it feels like being held by the universe itself, the way my grandfather once held a map: gently, reverently, as if the whole trembling world might fold itself into a small square and slip quietly into a glove compartment, waiting for morning, or maybe forever.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
